daily_brief: The fragile 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon entered its second day amid reports of violations and warnings from French President Macron that it 'may already be undermined' [BBC, RFI]. Iran reopened the Strait of Hormuz to commercial vessels for the duration of the truce, sending oil prices plunging from above $98 to $88 per barrel [BBC, France 24], though Tehran threatened to close the waterway again if the US blockade of Iranian ports continues [Al Jazeera, Globe and Mail]. President Trump declared a peace deal could come 'soon' but also warned the US would 'start dropping bombs again' if no agreement is reached by Wednesday [France 24, Al Jazeera]. In Europe, EU officials rushed to Budapest to fast-track cooperation with Hungary's Prime Minister-elect Péter Magyar, whose Tisza party ended Viktor Orbán's 16-year rule with a sweeping electoral victory [RFI, BBC]. The International Energy Agency warned that Europe has approximately six weeks of jet fuel remaining if Middle Eastern supplies are not restored [BBC]. Meanwhile, more than £1 billion was pledged for Sudan at a Berlin conference as the humanitarian crisis deepens [Guardian], and Australia and Japan formalized a major defense agreement worth AU$20 billion for frigate construction [Nikkei Asia].

top_stories.headline: Iran Reopens Strait of Hormuz During Lebanon Ceasefire, But Threatens Closure If US Blockade Continues
bullets: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi announced the Strait of Hormuz is open to all commercial vessels for the remainder of the 10-day Israel-Lebanon ceasefire, though military vessels remain banned
Oil prices fell sharply from above $98 to $88 per barrel following the announcement; global stock markets rallied with the S&P 500 closing at a record above 7100
Iran threatened to close the strait again if the US maintains its blockade of Iranian ports; Trump stated the blockade will continue until a deal is '100 percent complete'
Trump claimed a peace deal could come 'soon' but warned the US would 'start dropping bombs again' if no agreement is reached by Wednesday

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Focused on market impact and verification concerns from maritime groups

sources.name: France 24
sources.framing_note: Emphasized diplomatic context and Trump's optimistic statements

sources.name: Al Jazeera
sources.framing_note: Highlighted contradictions between US and Iranian messaging and Iran's conditions

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Emphasized uncertainty and unsuccessful vessel crossing attempts

sources.name: RNZ
sources.framing_note: Covered international diplomatic coordination including New Zealand's involvement

top_stories.headline: Israel-Lebanon Ceasefire Holds Amid Reported Violations and French Warnings
bullets: A 10-day US-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah took effect at midnight Thursday after six weeks of conflict that killed over 2,100 people and displaced more than one million in Lebanon
Lebanese army accused Israel of 'a number of violations' in southern Lebanon within hours of the truce beginning; French President Macron warned the ceasefire 'may already be undermined'
Israeli PM Netanyahu said Israeli troops would not withdraw and Israel would maintain a 10-kilometre security zone in southern Lebanon
Celebrations erupted in Lebanon with displaced residents beginning to return to the south, though many homes have been destroyed

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Balanced coverage of celebrations and obstacles to lasting peace

sources.name: RFI
sources.framing_note: Emphasized Macron's concerns and diplomatic fragility

sources.name: France 24
sources.framing_note: Focused on broader US-Iran diplomatic context

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Framed ceasefire as primarily about Trump's desire to end wider conflict

top_stories.headline: EU Rushes to Unlock €17 Billion for Hungary as Magyar Prepares to Take Power
bullets: EU officials met with Prime Minister-elect Péter Magyar's team in Budapest to fast-track cooperation and work toward unblocking approximately €17 billion in frozen EU funds
Magyar's Tisza party won 52% of the vote in the April 12 election, securing 140 of 199 parliamentary seats and ending Viktor Orbán's 16-year rule
Funds were frozen since 2022 over concerns about corruption, judicial independence, and rule of law under Orbán's government
Magyar plans to use his supermajority to retroactively limit prime ministerial terms to two, potentially blocking Orbán's return

sources.name: RFI
sources.framing_note: Focused on EU funding mechanics and timeline pressures

sources.name: BBC Europe
sources.framing_note: Emphasized Magyar's rapid moves to consolidate power and reform media

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Title framed Orbán's defeat as blow to 'reactionary international' (article content unavailable)

top_stories.headline: Europe Faces Jet Fuel Crisis With Six Weeks of Supply Remaining, IEA Warns
bullets: International Energy Agency executive director Fatih Birol warned Europe has 'maybe six weeks of jet fuel left' if Middle Eastern imports cannot be partially replaced
The Strait of Hormuz closure has disrupted the largest source of jet fuel to global markets; Gulf refineries supply much of global aviation fuel
IEA report noted that alternative suppliers like Korea, India, and China are themselves dependent on Middle Eastern crude
UK government stated airlines are 'currently not seeing disruption to supply' but is working with suppliers on contingency plans

sources.name: BBC Europe
sources.framing_note: Focused on potential flight cancellations and industry response

sources.name: Nikkei Asia
sources.framing_note: Covered related naphtha shortages affecting Japanese manufacturers despite government assurances

top_stories.headline: Over £1 Billion Pledged for Sudan at Berlin Conference as Humanitarian Crisis Deepens
bullets: International donors pledged £1.13 billion at a Berlin conference for Sudan, exceeding the $1 billion target set by German organizers
Three years of conflict have left 34 million people—two-thirds of Sudan's population—requiring humanitarian assistance in what the UN calls the world's largest humanitarian crisis
Neither Sudan's army nor the Rapid Support Forces attended the conference; Sudan's army-aligned foreign ministry accused Western countries of a 'colonial tutelage approach'
US senior adviser Massad Boulos said Washington is seeking a humanitarian truce and is 'not taking sides' in the war

sources.name: The Guardian
sources.framing_note: Emphasized protests against UAE's alleged RSF support and distant prospects for peace

additional_stories.headline: Australia and Japan sign AU$20 billion deal for Mitsubishi Heavy Industries to build frigates, deepening defense ties
China lodges 'serious protest' over New Zealand Air Force reconnaissance flights near its airspace, which NZDF denies disrupted civil aviation
Suspected Irish crime boss Daniel Kinahan arrested in Dubai under warrant from Irish courts
South African politician Julius Malema sentenced to five years in prison for firing rifle at 2018 political rally; appeal filed
Palestinian Authority extradites suspect in 1982 Paris Jewish restaurant attack that killed six people
Meta contractor Sama fires over 1,000 Kenyan workers after losing AI training contract
Kanye West concert in Poland cancelled following government pressure over antisemitic comments
US extends waiver allowing countries to purchase sanctioned Russian oil through May 16 to control energy prices
Pope Leo XIV celebrates Mass with 120,000 attendees in Cameroon, warns of AI dangers spreading 'polarisation and violence'
Turkish police detain 162 people over online posts about school shootings that killed nine this week
